Sequence Visualization and Pattern Comparison

  • Purpose
  • Sequence Plots
    • Heatmap
    • Index Plot
    • Distribution Plot
    • Custom Colors
  • Pattern Comparison
    • Reading Residuals
  • Test Choice

Purpose

This tutorial covers two related tasks:

  1. Visualizing raw sequences with sequence_plot().
  2. Comparing short sequence patterns with sequence_compare().

The first task is descriptive. The second task is comparative and can include statistical testing.

Sequence Plots

The trajectories data set contains engagement states for 138 learners across 15 time points.

data(trajectories)
dim(trajectories)
#> [1] 138  15
head(trajectories[, 1:8])
#>      1         2            3            4            5            6           
#> [1,] "Active"  "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Disengaged" "Active"     "Active"    
#> [2,] "Average" "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"    "Average"   
#> [3,] "Average" "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"    
#> [4,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Active"     "Average"   
#> [5,] "Active"  "Active"     "Active"     "Average"    "Active"     "Average"   
#> [6,] "Average" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Average"    "Disengaged" "Disengaged"
#>      7         8        
#> [1,] "Active"  "Active" 
#> [2,] "Average" "Average"
#> [3,] "Active"  "Active" 
#> [4,] "Average" "Active" 
#> [5,] "Active"  "Active" 
#> [6,] "Average" "Average"
table(as.vector(trajectories), useNA = "ifany")
#> 
#>     Active    Average Disengaged       <NA> 
#>        703        813        354        200

Heatmap

The default plot is a clustered heatmap. Rows are learners and columns are time points.

sequence_plot(trajectories)

The default sorting uses sequence distance. Other sorting rules change the question the plot answers.

sequence_plot(trajectories, sort = "frequency",
              main = "Rows sorted by state frequency")


sequence_plot(trajectories, sort = "start",
              main = "Rows sorted by first observed state")

Use k to show broad row groups from the dendrogram.

sequence_plot(
  trajectories,
  k = 3,
  k_color = "white",
  k_line_width = 2,
  main = "Sequence heatmap with three row groups"
)

Index Plot

The index plot removes the dendrogram and gives each sequence one row.

sequence_plot(trajectories, type = "index")

If you pass a net_clustering, sequence_plot() facets by cluster.

cl <- build_clusters(
  as.data.frame(trajectories),
  k = 3,
  dissimilarity = "hamming",
  method = "ward.D2"
)

sequence_plot(cl, type = "index")

Distribution Plot

The distribution view aggregates rows and shows how the state mix changes over time.

sequence_plot(trajectories, type = "distribution")

Use bars and count scale when the number of observed learners matters.

sequence_plot(
  trajectories,
  type = "distribution",
  geom = "bar",
  scale = "count"
)

The same view can be faceted by cluster.

sequence_plot(cl, type = "distribution")

Custom Colors

Use named colors so the mapping stays clear.

engagement_colors <- c(
  Active = "#2A9D8F",
  Average = "#E9C46A",
  Disengaged = "#E76F51"
)

sequence_plot(
  trajectories,
  k = 3,
  state_colors = engagement_colors,
  legend = "bottom",
  legend_title = "Engagement",
  main = "Student engagement trajectories"
)

Pattern Comparison

Sequence plots show the shape of the data. sequence_compare() tests whether short patterns are distributed differently across groups.

We compare early and late parts of each human-AI session. The split is within session, so the comparison asks whether patterns change over time inside the same sessions.

data(human_long)
dat <- human_long[order(human_long$session_id, human_long$order_in_session), ]

dat$position <- ave(dat$order_in_session, dat$session_id, FUN = seq_along)
dat$n_events <- ave(dat$order_in_session, dat$session_id, FUN = length)
dat$half <- ifelse(dat$position <= dat$n_events / 2, "early", "late")

table(dat$half)
#> 
#> early  late 
#>  5287  5509

Build a grouped network using the broad cluster action labels.

pattern_net <- build_network(
  dat,
  method = "relative",
  action = "cluster",
  actor = "session_id",
  time = "order_in_session",
  group = "half"
)

pattern_net
#> Group Networks (2 groups, group_col: half)
#> 
#>   Group  Nodes  Edges  Weights
#>   early  3      9      [0.105, 0.637]
#>   late   3      9      [0.131, 0.575]

Now compare 3- to 5-step patterns.

patterns <- sequence_compare(
  pattern_net,
  sub = 3:5,
  min_freq = 20,
  test = "chisq",
  adjust = "fdr"
)

patterns
#> Sequence Comparison  [118 patterns | 2 groups: early, late]
#>   Lengths: 3, 4, 5  |  min_freq: 20  |  chi-square  (fdr)
#> 
#>                                                     pattern length freq_early
#>              Directive->Directive->Directive->Metacognitive      4        278
#>  Directive->Directive->Directive->Metacognitive->Evaluative      5         97
#>                             Directive->Directive->Directive      3       1052
#>                 Metacognitive->Metacognitive->Metacognitive      3         32
#>   Directive->Directive->Directive->Directive->Metacognitive      5        142
#>             Directive->Directive->Metacognitive->Evaluative      4        114
#>   Directive->Directive->Directive->Metacognitive->Directive      5        120
#>                            Evaluative->Directive->Directive      3        265
#>  freq_late prop_early   prop_late resid_early resid_late statistic      p_value
#>        149 0.06876082 0.035100118    6.929245  -6.929245  47.32807 7.085431e-10
#>         25 0.02645214 0.006488451    6.849638  -6.849638  45.67504 8.235836e-10
#>        842 0.23672367 0.180803092    6.568847  -6.568847  42.81109 2.371385e-09
#>        102 0.00720072 0.021902512   -5.820751   5.820751  32.87528 2.898813e-07
#>         71 0.03872375 0.018427200    5.303063  -5.303063  27.38985 3.924716e-06
#>         51 0.02819688 0.012014134    5.271906  -5.271906  26.96981 4.064280e-06
#>         63 0.03272430 0.016350895    4.605937  -4.605937  20.53065 9.892452e-05
#>        379 0.05963096 0.081382865   -4.045115   4.045115  16.03382 9.177615e-04
#>  [ reached 'max' / getOption("max.print") -- omitted 2 rows ]
#>   ... and 108 more patterns
head(patterns$patterns, 12)

Reading Residuals

For a given pattern, a positive residual in a group means the pattern appears more often than expected in that group. A negative residual means it appears less often than expected.

The pyramid plot works for two groups.

plot(patterns, style = "pyramid", show_residuals = TRUE)

The heatmap is better when there are many groups or many patterns.

plot(patterns, style = "heatmap")

Sort by frequency when the most common patterns are the focus.

plot(patterns, style = "pyramid", sort = "frequency",
     show_residuals = TRUE)

Test Choice

Use test = "chisq" when the groups are not independent, such as early and late halves from the same sessions. Use test = "permutation" when group labels are exchangeable across independent sequences.

The plot is only a summary. Always inspect patterns$patterns before making a substantive claim.
